items.question: Who benefits from cross-validation for overfitting mitigation?
items.answer: Practitioners with limited labeled data—ML engineers comparing ridge vs random forest, researchers tuning SVM grids—use CV to avoid picking models that overfit one validation slice. Data scientists running GridSearchCV on train+val before a single test evaluation follow the same pattern.

items.question: What does CV for overfitting mitigation mean in practice?
items.answer: Rotate validation folds on the development set, rank models or hyperparameters by mean validation metric (and inspect std), use nested loops when many settings compete, then refit the winner on all development data. Fold mechanics are defined under Model Evaluation → Cross-Validation.

items.question: When should you use nested cross-validation instead of plain GridSearchCV?
items.answer: When you search many hyperparameters or model types and need an unbiased generalization estimate from the same development set—nested outer folds score settings chosen by inner CV. Plain GridSearchCV best_score_ is fine for ranking during development but optimistic as a final metric.

items.question: Where does mitigation-focused CV run relative to train, validation, and test?
items.answer: Run inner and outer CV on the development block (train+val) per Train/Val/Test Splits. Keep the locked test set untouched until after CV selects the model and hyperparameters, then evaluate the refit model once on test.

items.question: Why is low fold std important when picking a CV winner?
items.answer: High std across folds signals the model's performance swings with partition boundaries—often a sign of overfit-prone high variance. A slightly lower mean with much lower std may generalize more reliably than a lucky high mean on unstable folds.

items.question: How do you tune ridge alpha or random forest depth with CV in scikit-learn?
items.answer: Wrap the estimator in a Pipeline if preprocessing is needed, pass a KFold or StratifiedKFold splitter to GridSearchCV with a param grid (e.g. ridge__alpha or max_depth), inspect best_params_ and fold stability, set refit=True, then score once on a locked test set.

items.question: How does CV-based model selection compare to tuning on a single validation split?
items.answer: A single hold-out validation slice is fast but noisy when n is small—winners often overfit that slice. k-fold CV on train+val averages several partitions, reducing split luck. Hold-out suits large n; CV-based selection mitigates overfitting to one cut under scarcity.

items.question: How does nested CV differ from reporting GridSearchCV best_score_?
items.answer: GridSearchCV best_score_ is the mean inner CV metric of the winning hyperparameters—optimistic after searching many configs on the same folds. Nested CV adds an outer loop that scores each inner winner on held-out outer folds; report outer mean, not inner best_score_, for generalization.

items.question: What happens when you tune many hyperparameters on the same inner CV folds without nesting?
items.answer: Cause: dozens of settings ranked by shared fold scores. Effect: tuning optimism—the best inner mean overstates true generalization because the validation process was overfit to the search space.
